Configuration management deals with gathering, storing, and modifying the configurations of NEs. Administration activities cover, e.g., managing system users and their permissions and downloading and activating new SW releases to the NEs. Performance management deals with collecting, processing, and storing measurement data from the NEs and reporting on the collected data (e.g., revealing any adverse trends in some key performance indicators). Security management deals with things like controlling access to NEs, encrypting traffic between NMS and the NEs, and collecting audit logs from the NEs.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.169,
                "y": 0.471,
                "width": 0.711,
                "height": 0.17800000000000005,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 27,
                "region_id": 146,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 146,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 27
            },
            {
              "content": "NMS plays, if possible, an even more important role in CSPs' future with the advent of 5G technology, which complicates network management even further due to its new functionalities, such as network slicing. NMS should manage thousands of NEs from different telecom vendors with 'zero touch' operations (i.e. with minimum human intervention) to decrease costs for the CSPs and eliminate human errors. Data volumes coming from the managed networks are huge, and the NMS systems must scale to duly process and store all incoming data to ensure no data is lost.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.169,
                "y": 0.655,
                "width": 0.711,
                "height": 0.138,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 27,
                "region_id": 147,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 147,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 27
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;22&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.013000000000000012,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 28,
                "region_id": 148,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 148,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 28
            },
            {
              "content": "# 3 Methodologies",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.163,
                "y": 0.105,
                "width": 0.24699999999999997,
                "height": 0.015,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 28,
                "region_id": 149,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 149,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 28
            },
            {
              "content": "A systematic literature review was conducted based on guidelines defined in [15] and [16] to gather knowledge about the research topic. Also, a \"snowballing\" process was conducted based on guidelines in [17].",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.163,
                "y": 0.143,
                "width": 0.715,
                "height": 0.05100000000000002,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 28,
                "region_id": 150,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 150,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 28
            },
            {
              "content": "In this section, research questions are described (Section 3.1), and then in the next section, the implemented search strategy is reported (Section 3.2). After them, a quality assessment of included papers (Section 3.3) is described, and then data extraction methods such as used include and exclude criteria and analysis (Section 3.4).",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.163,
                "y": 0.202,
                "width": 0.715,
                "height": 0.09799999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 28,
                "region_id": 151,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 151,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 28
            },
            {
              "content": "## 3.1 Research Questions and Goal of the Research",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.163,
                "y": 0.32,
                "width": 0.608,
                "height": 0.015000000000000013,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 28,
                "region_id": 152,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 152,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 28
            },
            {
              "content": "The goal of this research is to compare messaging solutions based on a chosen criteria and to gain knowledge of MOMs for cloud native environment. The chosen criteria for the research is based on NMS's important characteristics in a cloud native environment. These characteristics are: implemented consumer-producer patterns, throughput, and supported security, reliability, scalability mechanisms, and backward compatibility. The chosen MOMs for the research are Apache Kafka, Apache Pulsar, and RabbitMQ.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.163,
                "y": 0.358,
                "width": 0.715,
                "height": 0.131,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 28,
                "region_id": 153,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 153,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 28
            },
            {
              "content": "Based on aforementioned requirements for the research, the research questions are formed:",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.163,
                "y": 0.496,
                "width": 0.715,
                "height": 0.03400000000000003,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 28,
                "region_id": 154,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 154,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 28
            },
            {
              "content": "*   Which consumer-producer patterns do Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ implement? (RQ₁).\n*   How do scalability mechanisms differ in Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ? (RQ₂).\n*   How does throughput differ in Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ? (RQ₃).\n*   How do reliability mechanisms differ in Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ? (RQ₄).\n*   Which security mechanisms do Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ support? (RQ₅).\n*   How does backward compatibility differ in Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ? (RQ₆).",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.198,
                "y": 0.547,
                "width": 0.6799999999999999,
                "height": 0.22299999999999998,
                "text": "list",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 28,
                "region_id": 155,
                "type": "list",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 155,
              "type": "list",
              "page": 28
            },
            {
              "content": "It is common knowledge in the industry that the most popular MOM in cloud native development is Kafka. Kafka's popularity in cloud native development is well earned. Kafka is by many characteristics outperforming traditional messaging brokers and was one of the first event brokers in the market.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.163,
                "y": 0.786,
                "width": 0.715,
                "height": 0.07699999999999996,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 28,
                "region_id": 156,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 156,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 28
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;23&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.013000000000000012,
                "height": 0.008999999999999994,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 29,
                "region_id": 157,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 157,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 29
            },
            {
              "content": "These MOMs were chosen for the research based on knowledge gathered from the industry of the best Kafka alternatives. Other alternatives also were considered, such as RocketMQ and NATS Streaming, but they were excluded because there was very little research. Also, including them would have enlarged the scope of this research too broad.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.077,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.09400000000000001,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 29,
                "region_id": 158,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 158,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 29
            },
            {
              "content": "## 3.2 Search strategy",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.191,
                "width": 0.24899999999999997,
                "height": 0.014999999999999986,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 29,
                "region_id": 159,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 159,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 29
            },
            {
              "content": "This section describes the search strategy, used search terms, used bibliographic sources, listing selected inclusion and exclusion criteria, and a selection process of inclusion for the gathered sources. Figure 11 illustrates the process for gathering the sources for this research.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.232,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.07099999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 29,
                "region_id": 160,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 160,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 29
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;img&gt;Figure 11 A process used for choosing the data included in the research.&lt;/img&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.237,
                "y": 0.595,
                "width": 0.583,
                "height": 0.016000000000000014,
                "text": "caption",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 29,
                "region_id": 161,
                "type": "caption",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 161,
              "type": "caption",
              "page": 29
            },
            {
              "content": "**Search terms.** The selection of used search terms was based on the chosen technologies, including their names and also any wording related to the characteristics for which the comparison was to be done against. The search string contained the following search terms:\n( (\"kafka\") OR (\"rabbitmq\") OR (\"apache pulsar\") ) AND ( (benchmark*) OR (secur*) OR (\"reliability\") OR (\"scalability\") OR (\"performance\") OR (\"throughput\") OR (pattern*) )",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.638,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.07399999999999995,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 29,
                "region_id": 162,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 162,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 29
            },
            {
              "content": "The asterisk character (*) was used to match any wording variations, for example, plurals and verb conjugations. The terms were matched against the title, abstract, and keywords of the paper to increase the likelihood of accurate results.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.777,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.05899999999999994,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 29,
                "region_id": 163,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 163,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 29
            },
            {
              "content": "**Bibliographic sources.** The most relevant bibliographic sources were selected based on suggestions in [16]. The sources were selected because they are commonly",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.844,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.040000000000000036,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 29,
                "region_id": 164,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 164,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 29
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;24&lt;/page_number&gt;\nknown as the most relevant sources for the software engineering domain. The list includes Scopus, IEEEXplore Digital Library, Springer, and Google Scholar.\n**Inclusion and exclusion criteria.** The inclusion and exclusion criteria were first applied to the title and abstract (T/A). Later it was applied to full text (Full) for included papers after reading the abstract. Table 1 shows how they were implemented based on the criteria.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.078,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.112,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 30,
                "region_id": 165,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 165,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 30
            },
            {
              "content": "**Table 1 Inclusion and exclusion criteria**",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.366,
                "y": 0.208,
                "width": 0.32399999999999995,
                "height": 0.013000000000000012,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 30,
                "region_id": 166,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 166,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 30
            },
            {
              "content": "<table>\n  <thead>\n    <tr>\n      <th>Criteria</th>\n      <th>Assessment criteria</th>\n      <th>Step</th>\n    </tr>\n  </thead>\n  <tbody>\n    <tr>\n      <td rowspan=\"3\">Inclusion</td>\n      <td>Comparison of the characteristics of chosen technologies</td>\n      <td>Full</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Architectural comparison of chosen technologies</td>\n      <td>Full</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Research on the future of any of chosen technologies</td>\n      <td>Full</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td rowspan=\"5\">Exclusion</td>\n      <td>Not in final stage</td>\n      <td>T/A</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Not written in English</td>\n      <td>T/A</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Published 2016 or earlier</td>\n      <td>T/A</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Research not applicable for cloud native</td>\n      <td>Full</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Paper done for marketing purposes</td>\n      <td>T/A</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td></td>\n      <td>Not peer reviewed</td>\n      <td>T/A</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td></td>\n      <td>Duplicate papers</td>\n      <td>Full</td>\n    </tr>\n  </tbody>\n</table>",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.238,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.22200000000000003,
                "text": "table",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 30,
                "region_id": 167,
                "type": "table",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 167,
              "type": "table",
              "page": 30
            },
            {
              "content": "**Search and selection process.** The search was conducted from September 2020 to January 2021, including all the papers available. The search string returned 241 unique papers.\n*Testing the applicability of inclusion and exclusion criteria.* Inclusion and exclusion criteria were tested on ten papers randomly selected from the retrieved papers.\n*Applying inclusion and exclusion criteria to title and abstract.* The inclusion and exclusion criteria were applied to all 241 unique papers. From all 241 papers, 19 papers were included after reading their title and abstract.\n*Full reading.* The included 19 papers were read fully. From them, 14 papers were excluded based on the excluding criteria, typically because they were not applicable for cloud native environment or had no comparison between two or more technologies. After a full reading, five papers were still included in the research.\n*Snowballing.* The snowballing process was performed on all the referenced papers in the included ones after full reading, ending up with one additional paper. Three papers were identified to be potentially included, but only one was selected after a full read.\nAfter the whole search process, six papers were included in the research, as reported in Table 2.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.485,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.367,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 30,
                "region_id": 168,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 168,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 30
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;25&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.515,
                "y": 0.046,
                "width": 0.01200000000000001,
                "height": 0.010000000000000002,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 31,
                "region_id": 169,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,

                }
              },
              "region_id": 169,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 31
            },
            {
              "content": "**Table 2** Results of search after applying inclusion and exclusion criteria",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.239,
                "y": 0.088,
                "width": 0.581,
                "height": 0.013000000000000012,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 31,
                "region_id": 170,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,

                }
              },
              "region_id": 170,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 31
            },
            {
              "content": "<table>\n  <thead>\n    <tr>\n      <th>Step</th>\n      <th># Papers</th>\n    </tr>\n  </thead>\n  <tbody>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Retrieval from bibliographic sources (unique papers)</td>\n      <td>241</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Reading by title and abstract</td>\n      <td>222 rejected</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Full reading</td>\n      <td>14 rejected</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Backward and forward snowballing</td>\n      <td>1 included</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Papers identified</td>\n      <td>6</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Primary studies</td>\n      <td>6</td>\n    </tr>\n  </tbody>\n</table>",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.222,
                "y": 0.116,
                "width": 0.619,
                "height": 0.14300000000000002,
                "text": "table",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 31,
                "region_id": 171,
                "type": "table",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 171,
              "type": "table",
              "page": 31
            },
            {
              "content": "## 3.3 Data extraction",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.288,
                "width": 0.237,
                "height": 0.014000000000000012,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 31,
                "region_id": 172,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 172,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 31
            },
            {
              "content": "Data were extracted from 6 primary studies, and a pie chart was created from the distribution of technologies in the included papers. Since most of the papers included more than one technology typically, the amounts do not add up to 6 papers. Figure 12 illustrates how many times any of the chosen MOMs are under research in the included papers.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.324,
                "width": 0.7070000000000001,
                "height": 0.09599999999999997,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 31,
                "region_id": 173,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 173,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 31
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;img&gt;A pie chart showing the distribution of chosen technologies in the included papers. The chart is divided into three sections: Kafka (6), RabbitMQ (4), and Pulsar (3). A legend below the chart indicates the colors for Kafka (purple), RabbitMQ (red), and Pulsar (yellow).&lt;/img&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.205,
                "y": 0.442,
                "width": 0.61,
                "height": 0.296,
                "text": "figure",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 31,
                "region_id": 174,
                "type": "figure",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 174,
              "type": "figure",
              "page": 31
            },
            {
              "content": "**Figure 12** Distribution of the chosen technologies in the included papers.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.24,
                "y": 0.762,
                "width": 0.575,
                "height": 0.014000000000000012,
                "text": "caption",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 31,
                "region_id": 175,
                "type": "caption",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 175,
              "type": "caption",
              "page": 31
            },
            {
              "content": "In general, the search process aimed to have every included paper some benchmarking or comparison results between two or more selected technologies. This requirement is the reason why there are only six papers left. This thesis aims to compare the selected technologies one against the another and not compare the features of a single technology.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.802,
                "width": 0.7070000000000001,
                "height": 0.09599999999999997,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 31,
                "region_id": 176,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 176,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 31
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;26&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.014000000000000012,
                "height": 0.008999999999999994,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 32,
                "region_id": 177,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 177,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 32
            },
            {
              "content": "The reason why so few of the papers did include Pulsar is, as we will see later, it is quite new technology. RabbitMQ and Kafka are considerably older, hence having much more papers done research using them.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.077,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.05500000000000001,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 32,
                "region_id": 178,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 178,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 32
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;27&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.517,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.014000000000000012,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 33,
                "region_id": 179,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 179,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 33
            },
            {
              "content": "# 4 Technologies",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.105,
                "width": 0.22,
                "height": 0.016,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 33,
                "region_id": 180,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 180,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 33
            },
            {
              "content": "Comparing MOMs to each other is a non-trivial task. The best choice is whatever fits your use case. Typically, every MOM aims for a certain type of messaging that makes them good at one thing and worse at another. There is no general comparison between the MOMs because there are many different variables that affect the results when making comparison. For example, these variables are the server's underlying hardware and the configuration settings of the MOM. In the next section, typical differences between MOMs are described in which this research is concerned. Some of the features are closely related to each other. For example, data replication is related to reliability and scalability.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.143,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.17800000000000002,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 33,
                "region_id": 181,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 181,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 33
            },
            {
              "content": "## 4.1 Comparing MOMs by defined characteristics",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.34,
                "width": 0.604,
                "height": 0.014999999999999958,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 33,
                "region_id": 182,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 182,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 33
            },
            {
              "content": "**Patterns.** All the MOMs simplify connections inside the system by implementing different messaging topologies such as point-to-point and pub/sub. There are two types of communication between a MOM and a client, push-based and pull-based communication. Implementations in MOMs that use push method to the consumer, could without any backup logic lead into a consumer becoming overwhelmed by the amount of data it receives, in essence into a type of Denial of Service Attack situation. This research will compare available **messaging patterns** in the MOMs, and **push/pull implementations** between MOM and client.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.375,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.15200000000000002,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 33,
                "region_id": 183,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 183,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 33
            },
            {
              "content": "**Scalability.** MOMs implement load balancing and replication by sending metadata about connected consumers and producers. MOM aims to implement a proper load balancing between the producers and consumers and scale out when needed to avoid bottlenecks. The ability to configure buffering for the messages and streaming is essential functionality for a modern MOM. The broker storage is also closely in connection with the reliability of the MOM. For example, replication can be done on the broker level or data level. By leveraging replication on the broker level by replicating broker instances, broker faults do not endanger the system's whole functionality. Data replication replicates the data across the broker instances to increase fault tolerance so that data will not be lost even if one broker instance is lost. Data and broker replication is also an essential aspect of scalability. In case of an increased message load, increasing the number of broker instances will prevent bottlenecks in the system. This research will take a close look at **data replication mechanism** and **broker replication mechanism** of each MOM.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.534,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.2839999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 33,
                "region_id": 184,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 184,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 33
            },
            {
              "content": "**Throughput.** Throughput in the context of MOMs is generally defined as the amount of messages flowing through the broker. The factors that affect throughput",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.825,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.041000000000000036,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 33,
                "region_id": 185,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 185,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 33
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;28&lt;/page_number&gt;\nare typically related to reliability mechanisms and scalability mechanisms. There are also differences between brokers in how they are able to handle different message sizes and how that affects the latency of the system. Also, the filtering of the messages is important, and that implementation can be located in the broker or at the client depending on the broker, which will also affect performance overall. Sending the messages in compressed or decompressed form also has a significant effect on the performance. This research will compare effects of scaling mechanisms, reliability mechanisms, and message size to **latency** and **throughput**. Also available **compression types** are compared for each technology.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.031,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.225,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 34,
                "region_id": 186,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 186,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 34
            },
            {
              "content": "**Reliability.** Reliability in MOMs is closely related to message persistence and durability. Traditional MOMs don’t always implement durability, meaning that messages can be lost, and the messages can become unordered in case of no active receivers at the receiving moment. Traditional MOMs tend to save received messages into their local memory, and modern MOMs usually have the functionality to save them into persistent storage. [9] Also, message delivery guarantees and message ordering are essential to increase reliability. Delivery guarantees differ from broker to broker, but typically most of them support at-least-once and at-most-once deliveries. Strict message ordering typically has a decreasing performance effect on MOMs. This research will compare **message persistence**, **message durability**, **message delivery guarantees** and **message ordering** of each MOM.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.259,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.21599999999999997,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 34,
                "region_id": 187,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 187,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 34
            },
            {
              "content": "**Security.** The main security features for MOMs are encryption and authentication. Encryption ensures that messages between clients and MOM are secured with an encryption mechanism. Typical encryption mechanisms for MOMs are Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) and Transport Layer Security (TLS), usually mentioned as SSL/TLS. Authentication ensures that only wanted clients can create a connection to the MOM. The connection can be established with a secure and reliable authentication mechanism such as Simple Authentication and Security Layer (SASL). Both security mechanisms can affect the system performance, especially throughput. This research will compare available **encryption** and **authentication** mechanisms for each MOM.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.479,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.20200000000000007,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 34,
                "region_id": 188,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 188,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 34
            },
            {
              "content": "**Backward compatibility.** Typically all the MOMs promise that their systems are backward compatible, depending on the versioning. Anyway, even the smallest changes to the API can require refactoring and bugs. Rolling upgrade is essential for systems promising high availability, meaning an ability to upgrade the broker version without generating downtime in the system. This research will compare implementations of **rolling update** and whether **backward compatibility** is guaranteed between versions in each MOM.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.685,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.1419999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 34,
                "region_id": 189,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 189,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 34
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;29&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.514,
                "y": 0.046,
                "width": 0.013000000000000012,
                "height": 0.010000000000000002,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 35,
                "region_id": 190,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 190,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 35
            },
            {
              "content": "## 4.2 Kafka",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.089,
                "width": 0.126,
                "height": 0.012999999999999998,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 35,
                "region_id": 191,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 191,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 35
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka is an event streaming platform written in Scala. Kafka was open sourced in 2011 and is being developed and maintained by Apache Software Foundation. Kafka was initiated at LinkedIn to replace traditional message brokers, which were complicated to scale. Kafka is developed to aim for low latency and high throughput with large volumes of data, meaning it favors performance over reliability features.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.126,
                "width": 0.7050000000000001,
                "height": 0.096,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 35,
                "region_id": 192,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 192,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 35
            },
            {
              "content": "Traditional message brokers tend to slow down when adding multiple consumers into a topic and queue. Kafka solves this by a different architectural approach that makes it easier to scale the broker horizontally. Kafka cluster's architecture is two-tiered, which includes a Kafka broker and a ZooKeeper server. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.228,
                "width": 0.7070000000000001,
                "height": 0.07499999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 35,
                "region_id": 193,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 193,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 35
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.2.1 Patterns",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.326,
                "width": 0.178,
                "height": 0.01200000000000001,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 35,
                "region_id": 194,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 194,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 35
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka supports two messaging patterns by leveraging topics, point-to-point, and publish/subscribe. To utilize parallel processing to increase performance, consumers can be separated into consumer groups. The consumer groups can consume a single topic at the same time. Consumer groups manage their message offsets, telling where it is currently reading the data in the partition. Kafka replicates the topic partitions and tries to assign each partition to one consumer in the consumer group. Since only one consumer in the consumer group can read the messages of a single partition, increasing performance is done by adding more partitions and consumers. By leveraging independent offsets, in case of a single consumer, a topic acts as a queue and with multiple consumers as a pub/sub topic. [12] Figure 13 illustrates the architecture of the Kafka broker.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.362,
                "width": 0.7070000000000001,
                "height": 0.21799999999999997,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 35,
                "region_id": 195,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 195,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 35
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;img&gt;Kafka cluster (many machines) diagram showing Producers sending data to Brokers, which then distribute the data to Consumers. The diagram shows multiple producers, multiple brokers, and multiple consumers. The consumers are grouped into \"Consumer 1 of group\" and \"Consumer 2 of group\".&lt;/img&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.244,
                "y": 0.6,
                "width": 0.5640000000000001,
                "height": 0.22399999999999998,
                "text": "figure",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 35,
                "region_id": 196,
                "type": "figure",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 196,
              "type": "figure",
              "page": 35
            },
            {
              "content": "*Figure 13 An architecture of a Kafka broker.*",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.345,
                "y": 0.836,
                "width": 0.363,
                "height": 0.013000000000000012,
                "text": "caption",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 35,
                "region_id": 197,
                "type": "caption",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 197,
              "type": "caption",
              "page": 35
            },
            {
              "content": "Each consumer group can have multiple instances of a particular type of con-",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.204,
                "y": 0.877,
                "width": 0.672,
                "height": 0.014000000000000012,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 35,
                "region_id": 198,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 198,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 35
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;30&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.517,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.016000000000000014,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 36,
                "region_id": 199,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 199,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 36
            },
            {
              "content": "sumer in the group, but only the lastly joined one into the group will continue consuming messages. The ability to transform a topic into a queue simplifies connections since there is no need to keep track of e.g., which of the consumers should get the next message in a round-robin fashion like in traditional message brokers. In addition, there is no need to keep track of which consumer the earlier messages were delivered, in case of redelivery.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.169,
                "y": 0.09,
                "width": 0.716,
                "height": 0.11699999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 36,
                "region_id": 200,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 200,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 36
            },
            {
              "content": "In Kafka, consumers pull data from brokers. When consumer falls behind using pull-based connection, consumer will catch up later, since it manages its own offset in that partition. These all things are an example of transferring responsibility from broker to client. It increases broker performance but needs to be taken into account in client code. As typically with all the pull-based systems, Kafka leverages aggressive batching of data. [12] Kafka offers streaming by Kafka Streams API. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.195,
                "width": 0.728,
                "height": 0.123,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 36,
                "region_id": 201,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 201,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 36
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.2.2 Scalability mechanisms",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.335,
                "width": 0.36,
                "height": 0.01699999999999996,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 36,
                "region_id": 202,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 202,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 36
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka brokers are connected to ZooKeeper servers that provide high availability by replicating information across the system. ZooKeeper manages the distribution of partitions of a topic between brokers in the cluster. ZooKeeper assigns master and slave roles for broker instances in the cluster. The master broker receives the messages sent into a topic and is responsible for replicating the messages among the slave brokers. These distributed partitions are called 'replicas', and a broker containing all of the messages and partitions also held by the leader is called an *in-sync replica*. There is ongoing work to replace ZooKeeper with a self-managed metadata quorum. [21] Figure 14 illustrates how Kafka brokers are replicated in the cluster.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.158,
                "y": 0.373,
                "width": 0.73,
                "height": 0.19699999999999995,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 36,
                "region_id": 203,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 203,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 36
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;31&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.519,
                "y": 0.033,
                "width": 0.013000000000000012,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 37,
                "region_id": 204,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 204,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 37
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;img&gt;Diagram showing Kafka broker replication. It includes a Producer and Consumer sending messages to a Broker leader. The Broker leader, Broker follower, and another Broker follower are all connected to a ZooKeeper. Each broker has a Serving component, a Message, and a Storage component. Dashed lines indicate connections between the brokers and ZooKeeper.&lt;/img&gt;\nFigure 14 Replication in Kafka brokers.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.238,
                "y": 0.072,
                "width": 0.582,
                "height": 0.313,
                "text": "figure",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 37,
                "region_id": 205,
                "type": "figure",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 205,
              "type": "figure",
              "page": 37
            },
            {
              "content": "By leveraging in-sync replicas, in the case of a master broker going offline, the in-sync replica will take over the master role. Slave brokers can send acknowledgment receipts, which defines how many in-sync replicas must send a receipt about a successful replication before the application thread continues. For example, replication can be three, meaning one master and two slave brokers own replicas. It is suggested to set a minimum amount of in-sync replicas to two. Thereby one in-sync replica broker going offline does not affect the whole system. Using these in-sync replicas, Kafka minimizes the need to write messages from memory to disk to speed up the throughput. [9] All the scalability features in Kafka are closely related to fault tolerance and high availability. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.438,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.202,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 37,
                "region_id": 206,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 206,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 37
            },
            {
              "content": "Data replication between multiple Kafka brokers in different geographical locations can be done using a command line tool MirrorMaker, which is shipped by default with Kafka. The keys in a topic need to understandable by MirrorMaker to ensure that ordering is sustained between brokers since partitions could be different between the two brokers per topic when scaling horizontally. [9] MirrorMaker is the tool that will be used when implementing geo-replication.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.644,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.11499999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 37,
                "region_id": 207,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 207,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 37
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.2.3 Reliability mechanisms",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.777,
                "width": 0.361,
                "height": 0.018000000000000016,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 37,
                "region_id": 208,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 208,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 37
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka enables retaining and replaying the messages from an append-only log file, in which Kafka stores its messages, making them persistent and durable. Kafka does not remove the messages from the storage after they are consumed. Kafka retains by",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.815,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.06000000000000005,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 37,
                "region_id": 209,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 209,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 37
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;32&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.514,
                "y": 0.046,
                "width": 0.013000000000000012,
                "height": 0.010000000000000002,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 210,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 210,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "default all the messages for one week. After that, it removes the messages from the storage, regardless they are consumed or not. [9] Kafka leverages zero-copy principle for delivering data from the log file to the network socket, skipping the application layer, saving CPU resources.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.09,
                "width": 0.709,
                "height": 0.07600000000000001,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 211,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 211,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "When using replication, Kafka will save the messages into replicated partitions of the log. In case of a failure, Kafka will be able to recover from those replicas. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.173,
                "width": 0.709,
                "height": 0.035,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 212,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 212,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka offers three different message guarantees: at-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once. At-most-once does not guarantee message delivery, hence offering the best performance. At-least-once can lead to duplicate messages if acknowledgment is not delivered to the producer back. Exactly-once is implemented using transactions, meaning that messages are sent in batches, and they are all successfully deliver or not at all. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.214,
                "width": 0.709,
                "height": 0.11700000000000002,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 213,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 213,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "Every topic in Kafka has its own log, which consists of one or more partitions, in which the received messages are stored in order. The ordering is guaranteed within each partition, meaning that ordering is not guaranteed between partitions.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.338,
                "width": 0.709,
                "height": 0.05399999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 214,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 214,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka supports four different compression algorithms: Gzip, Snappy, 14z, and Zstandard. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.399,
                "width": 0.709,
                "height": 0.034999999999999976,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 215,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 215,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.2.4 Security mechanisms",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.455,
                "width": 0.31999999999999995,
                "height": 0.01599999999999996,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 216,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 216,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka supports mixes between authorized and unauthorized, as well as encrypted and non-encrypted setups. Kafka supports SSL/TLS encryption between broker-client, broker-broker, and broker-cli. Authentication between broker and client is supported using SSL/TLS, SASL, and Kerberos. Also, authentication between Kafka and ZooKeeper is available with SSL/TLS. By leveraging authorization using access control lists (ACL), Kafka can only allow certain users to write to or read from specific topics. Every Kafka broker should have their SSL/TLS certificate generated with an individual SSL/TLS key. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.491,
                "width": 0.709,
                "height": 0.15800000000000003,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 217,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 217,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.2.5 Backward compatibility",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.67,
                "width": 0.356,
                "height": 0.016000000000000014,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 218,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 218,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka is designed to be an always-on system that can be upgraded to a newer version without downtime using rolling update by guaranteeing backward compatibility with earlier versions. After broker upgrade, the Kafka clients need to be updated to newer version, but upgrading the broker first will not break the system. With newer versions of Kafka, it is also possible to upgrade clients before the broker, making them forward compatible. Implementing this needs special effort from a schema management point of view, but according to documentation, it is possible. In the documentation, there exist great step-by-step tutorials for implementing a rolling update. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.707,
                "width": 0.709,
                "height": 0.17700000000000005,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 38,
                "region_id": 219,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 219,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 38
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;33&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.514,
                "y": 0.046,
                "width": 0.014000000000000012,
                "height": 0.010000000000000002,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 39,
                "region_id": 220,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 220,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 39
            },
            {
              "content": "## 4.3 Pulsar",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.089,
                "width": 0.131,
                "height": 0.012999999999999998,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 39,
                "region_id": 221,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 221,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 39
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar is a high-performance messaging, and streaming platform that originated in Yahoo! in 2014, but its development is nowadays powered mainly by Chinese companies. Pulsar is mostly written Java and has been open sourced in 2016. It is now developed and maintained by Apache Software Foundation. Yahoo! developed Pulsar for the same need as Kafka has been developed, to replace traditional message brokers. Pulsar is also developed for high throughput and low latency with massive amounts of data. This approach always comes with a downside of reduced security and reliability features when comparing to traditional brokers. When the development started for Pulsar, Kafka was already open sourced, so Kafka's best parts were picked and some parts implemented differently that seemed not optimal. Pulsar cluster's architecture is three-layered, which includes Pulsar broker, BookKeeper server, and ZooKeeper server. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.125,
                "width": 0.7070000000000001,
                "height": 0.241,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 39,
                "region_id": 222,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 222,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 39
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.3.1 Patterns",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.387,
                "width": 0.179,
                "height": 0.013000000000000012,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 39,
                "region_id": 223,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 223,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 39
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar supports pub/sub messaging pattern, which is based on topics. Consumers can make different kinds of subscriptions to these topics, which modify how the consumers communicate with the topic. Four different subscriptions are available: exclusive, failover, shared, and key_shared. By combining these subscriptions, different kinds of patterns are achievable, such as point-to-point. By combining these subscriptions, implementing Kafka-like consumer groups is also possible. [19] When a consumer connects to a topic, it specifies the subscription it is using, the subscription keeps track of the point at which the consumer is reading the topic. Figure 15 illustrates these subscriptions and their impact on consumer behavior.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.423,
                "width": 0.708,
                "height": 0.178,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 39,
                "region_id": 224,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 224,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 39
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;img&gt;Diagram showing Pulsar topic subscriptions. Two producers (Producer 1, Producer 2) send messages to a Pulsar topic. The topic is connected to four subscription types: Exclusive (Subscription A -> Consumer A), Failover (Subscription B -> Consumer B-1, Consumer B-2 with a dashed line indicating failover), Shared (Subscription C -> Consumer C-1, Consumer C-2 with labels <k1, v1> and <k1, v2>), and Key_Shared (Subscription D -> Consumer D-1, Consumer D-2 with labels <k1, v1> and <k2, v2>).&lt;/img&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.218,
                "y": 0.62,
                "width": 0.619,
                "height": 0.23199999999999998,
                "text": "figure",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 39,
                "region_id": 225,
                "type": "figure",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 225,
              "type": "figure",
              "page": 39
            },
            {
              "content": "*Figure 15 Pulsar topic subscriptions.*",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.375,
                "y": 0.865,
                "width": 0.30200000000000005,
                "height": 0.01200000000000001,
                "text": "caption",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 39,
                "region_id": 226,
                "type": "caption",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 226,
              "type": "caption",
              "page": 39
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;34&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.01200000000000001,
                "height": 0.008999999999999994,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 227,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 227,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 40
            },
