            {
              "content": "Exclusive subscription means that only one consumer is allowed to create a connection to the topic, rejecting all the other consumers from creating a connection.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.076,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.035,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 228,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 228,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "content": "Failover subscription means that multiple consumers can create a connection to a topic, but only one of them can consume. In case of the consuming service disconnecting from the topic, the next oldest connected service will continue consuming.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.115,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.05699999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 229,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 229,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "content": "A shared subscription means that multiple consumers can create a connection to a topic, and they all can consume messages from it in a round-robin fashion. By using a shared subscription, the application can scale horizontally since the messages can be processed in parallel by multiple consumers.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.178,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.07700000000000001,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 230,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 230,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "content": "Key_shared subscriptions work just like the shared one, but the messages are always delivered to the same consumer, based on the key in the message, rather than using round robin.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.261,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.05499999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 231,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 231,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar aggressively batches the data as typical when aiming for high throughput. By default, Pulsar waits for an acknowledgment by the consumer that the message was processed. When using batching, the batch is acknowledged only when the consumer acknowledges all the messages of the batch.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.321,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.07600000000000001,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 232,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 232,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar also has a feature called chunking that cannot be run simultaneously with batching. Chunking splits the messages when a message exceeds the defined size. Pulsar implements streaming of data using Pulsar Functions. Pulsar implements push-based communication between broker and consumer, but the API is designed to simulate pull-based communication. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.402,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.09799999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 233,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 233,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.3.2 Scalability mechanisms",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.515,
                "width": 0.363,
                "height": 0.018000000000000016,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 234,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 234,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar can effectively replicate broker instances horizontally leveraging ZooKeeper, just like Kafka. ZooKeeper cluster handles the coordination of tasks between Pulsar broker instances when they are replicated. ZooKeeper also plays an important role when implementing geo-replication, which is a default functionality of a Pulsar cluster. Pulsar uses ZooKeeper also as metadata storage for cluster configuration.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.552,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.09799999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 235,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 235,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "content": "In addition to traditional topic-based pub/sub, Pulsar also implements Kafka like partitioned topics. Normal topics in Pulsar can only be used with a single broker instance, meaning partitioned topics must be used when there are multiple brokers in the cluster. [19] Figure 16 illustrates how partitioned topics work in Pulsar.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.655,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.07699999999999996,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 40,
                "region_id": 236,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 236,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;35&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.515,
                "y": 0.046,
                "width": 0.01200000000000001,
                "height": 0.010000000000000002,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 41,
                "region_id": 237,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 237,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 41
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;img&gt;Diagram of a Pulsar cluster showing a Producer, Broker 1, Broker 2, Broker 3, Consumer 1, and Consumer 2. The Producer has partitions P0, P1, P2, P3, P4. Broker 1 has partitions P0, P3. Broker 2 has partitions P1, P4. Broker 3 has partition P2. Consumer 1 has partitions P0, P1, P2, P3, P4. Consumer 2 has partitions P0, P1, P2, P3, P4. Arrows show the routing of messages from Producer partitions to Broker partitions and from Broker partitions to Consumer partitions. Text below the Producer reads \"Routing mode determines which internal topic a message should be published to\". Text below the Consumer reads \"Subscription mode determines which consumers a message should be delivered to\".&lt;/img&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.226,
                "y": 0.089,
                "width": 0.604,
                "height": 0.33399999999999996,
                "text": "figure",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 41,
                "region_id": 238,
                "type": "figure",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 238,
              "type": "figure",
              "page": 41
            },
            {
              "content": "*Figure 16 An example of partitioned topics in a Pulsar cluster.*",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.273,
                "y": 0.442,
                "width": 0.512,
                "height": 0.014000000000000012,
                "text": "caption",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 41,
                "region_id": 239,
                "type": "caption",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 239,
              "type": "caption",
              "page": 41
            },
            {
              "content": "These partitions are spread across the replicated broker instances to increase throughput. These partitions also have different kinds of routing modes that configure the way the topic partition is done on brokers. Pulsar automatically handles the partitioning of topics to the brokers. [19] Most of the scalability features are closely related to fault tolerance and high availability. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.483,
                "width": 0.706,
                "height": 0.09599999999999997,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 41,
                "region_id": 240,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 240,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 41
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.3.3 Reliability mechanisms",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.6,
                "width": 0.34400000000000003,
                "height": 0.016000000000000014,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 41,
                "region_id": 241,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 241,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 41
            },
            {
              "content": "By default, Pulsar retains the messages if there are no consumers at the receiving time, making the messages durable. Pulsar only stores by default all unacknowledged messages into persistent storage, making the messages persistent. Pulsar deletes by default all the acknowledged messages, but this can be changed from the configuration settings. There is also a possibility to use non-persistent topics, meaning the messages are only saved into the memory, meaning they are not persistent. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.637,
                "width": 0.7070000000000001,
                "height": 0.137,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 41,
                "region_id": 242,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 242,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 41
            },
            {
              "content": "In Pulsar cluster, BookKeeper take care of handling the data in the cluster. Pulsar broker handles incoming and outgoing messaging between broker and client. Pulsar uses BookKeeper as storage to save and retain messages to make them persistent. BookKeeper cluster consists of bookies that write to one or more of append-only data structures called ledgers, in which the messages are stored. The ledger is from an architectural point of view somewhat equal to the log file in Kafka. The ledger",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.781,
                "width": 0.7070000000000001,
                "height": 0.11599999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 41,
                "region_id": 243,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 243,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 41
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;36&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.519,
                "y": 0.034,
                "width": 0.01200000000000001,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 42,
                "region_id": 244,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 244,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 42
            },
            {
              "content": "consists of segments that are somewhat equal to partitions of a log file in Kafka, but the segments are written as an ordered list and not concurrently like in Kafka. This segmentation of a ledger means that Pulsar writes only to the last segment, meaning the earlier segments are immutable. [19] Figure 17 illustrates the architecture of the Pulsar cluster.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.076,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.09599999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 42,
                "region_id": 245,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 245,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 42
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;img&gt;Figure 17 An example of architecture of a Pulsar cluster.&lt;/img&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.285,
                "y": 0.188,
                "width": 0.48700000000000004,
                "height": 0.2,
                "text": "figure",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 42,
                "region_id": 246,
                "type": "figure",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 246,
              "type": "figure",
              "page": 42
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar offers a tiered storage functionality that allows older data to be sent from BookKeeper to a long-term and cheaper cloud storage such as Amazon S3. Since segments are written one by one as an ordered list, Pulsar can offload older immutable segments stored in the ledger one by one to the cloud storage. Even though the older segments are offloaded to the cloud storage, Pulsar can query that data using Pulsar SQL. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.444,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.11300000000000004,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 42,
                "region_id": 247,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 247,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 42
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar supports three message guarantees: at-most-once, at-least-once, and effectively-once. Effectively-once means that each message that is sent to a function, the function returns an output that is associated with the input message. That means Pulsar does not implement exactly-once guarantee, even though Pulsar supports transactions like Kafka. Transactions in Pulsar are applicable only for streaming and have a little different intend of use. The parallelism of consumption in exclusive and failover subscriptions is limited to the number of partitions in the topic. Parallelism can go beyond the number of partitions when using the shared subscription, but message ordering is not guaranteed.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.563,
                "width": 0.756,
                "height": 0.18300000000000005,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 42,
                "region_id": 248,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 248,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 42
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar offers a strict ordering guarantee for messages per partition in the topic when using exclusive or failover subscriptions. The ordering guarantee can be implemented per partition or per producer, but typically per partition functionality is used. When a client subscribes to multiple topics, the message ordering is not guaranteed. It is guaranteed only for a single topic subscription. Also, Pulsar has a functionality to guarantee that messages are not saved to the persistent storage",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.751,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.121,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 42,
                "region_id": 249,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 249,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 42
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;37&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.013000000000000012,
                "height": 0.008999999999999994,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 250,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 250,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "multiple times. Meaning that no duplicate messages can be saved no matter which message guarantee is used, but it does not guarantee the consumer will not consume the message multiple times.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.077,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.054000000000000006,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 251,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 251,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar supports four different compression algorithms: zlib, Snappy, 14z, and Zstandard. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.138,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.034999999999999976,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 252,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 252,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.3.4 Security mechanisms",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.191,
                "width": 0.33199999999999996,
                "height": 0.015999999999999986,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 253,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 253,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "By default, Pulsar does not use any encryption, authentication, or authorization features. Pulsar supports Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) end-to-end encryption from client to storage level. Encryption for communication between client-broker, broker-broker and broker-cli is supported with SSL/TLS. Like Kafka, by leveraging SSL/TLS keys, SSL/TLS certificates can be assigned to each broker.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.232,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.092,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 254,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 254,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar broker validates credentials when a client tries to connect to the broker. After authentication is done, the broker creates a role token that the connection is valid for a certain time. Pulsar offers four authentication methods: SSL/TLS, SASL, Athenz, Kerberos, and JSON Web Token Authentication.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.33,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.07699999999999996,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 255,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 255,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar uses access control lists to implement authorization for clients based on the role tokens assigned in the authentication phase. Also, network segmentation can be used. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.413,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.05500000000000005,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 256,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 256,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.3.5 Backward compatibility",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.487,
                "width": 0.37,
                "height": 0.015000000000000013,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 257,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 257,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "According to Pulsar documentation, broker and client versions are backward compatible as well as forward compatible. Pulsar documentation has a great amount of tutorials for implementing canary testing as well as rolling update. Pulsar documentation suggests testing your upgrade with canary tests always first. Typically, there is no need to upgrade ZooKeeper instances, the broker and bookies are enough. When implementing rolling update, there needs to be taken into account, that bookies are stateful and broker is stateless. To implement the rolling update, brokers and bookies need to be upgraded one-by-one to not have any downtime. The upgrade is done by simply stopping the broker or bookie, upgrading it, and then restarting with few commands. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.522,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.19699999999999995,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 258,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 258,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.4 RabbitMQ",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.74,
                "width": 0.19799999999999998,
                "height": 0.016000000000000014,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 259,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 259,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ is a feature-rich traditional message broker originally started by Rabbit Technologies in 2007 but is since 2013 developed and maintained by VMware. RabbitMQ’s source code is published under Mozilla Public License, and its written in Erlang. RabbitMQ supports multiple protocols to implement messaging: Advanced Messaging Queuing Protocol (AMQP), Streaming Text Oriented Messaging",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.776,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.10399999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 43,
                "region_id": 260,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 260,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 43
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;38&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.514,
                "y": 0.046,
                "width": 0.014000000000000012,
                "height": 0.010000000000000002,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 44,
                "region_id": 261,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 261,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 44
            },
            {
              "content": "Protocol (STOMP), Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (MQTT), and Hyper-\ntext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) using WebSockets. RabbitMQ was developed using\nAMQP 0-9-1, which is its core technology still to this day. Other technologies are\navailable only by using a plugin. AMQP 1.0 and its latter versions are also available\nthrough a plugin because they share essentially nothing with the RabbitMQ's core\nversion. RabbitMQ has improved the AMQP 0-9-1 by extending it from various\nfunctional aspects. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.075,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.137,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 44,
                "region_id": 262,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 262,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 44
            },
            {
              "content": "AMQP is a binary protocol with rich features. It is implemented favoring security\nand reliability features over performance. The most significant benefit of AMQP is\nits interoperability between different vendors and proven reliability. AMQP is a\nwire-level protocol, meaning that it describes only the format of data that will be\nsent, making systems interoperable that are implemented in different languages.\n[20] The only messaging protocol to be taken under research is the core version of\nRabbitMQ.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.219,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.13399999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 44,
                "region_id": 263,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 263,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 44
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.4.1 Patterns",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.171,
                "y": 0.393,
                "width": 0.17999999999999997,
                "height": 0.013999999999999957,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 44,
                "region_id": 264,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 264,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 44
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ supports point-to-point and pub/sub messaging patterns. As its name\nsuggests, RabbitMQ started as a message queue, extended with pub/sub later on.\nRabbitMQ has four important terms that are used in the broker architecture. Ex-\nchanges are endpoints in the RabbitMQ broker, which the clients connect to send\nmessages. Bindings are used to link the exchanges to queues, handling the routing\nlogic depending on the binding key defined in the queue. Queues are components\nthat handle message delivery to receivers and store messages by saving them into\npersistent storage. Virtual hosts are used to separating exchanges, queues, and users\ninto groups to ease administration and access control. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.412,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.174,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 44,
                "region_id": 265,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 265,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 44
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ supports four different exchange types. Direct exchange delivers the\nmessage into a queue defined by the binding key in the message header. Topic\nexchange delivers the message to the queues based on a routing filter defined between\nthe exchange and the queue. Fanout exchange delivers the message to all bounded\nqueues to the exchange, also known as a broadcast. Also, a headers exchange is\nsupported, which bases the routing logic between the exchange and the queues on\nother message header attributes than the routing key. The difference between the\nrouting key and the binding key is, that the routing key is defined in the message,\nwhereas the binding key is defined in the queue. [20] Figure 18 illustrates how\ndifferent exchange types work in RabbitMQ broker.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.595,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.20100000000000007,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 44,
                "region_id": 266,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 266,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 44
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;39&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.519,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.01200000000000001,
                "height": 0.008999999999999994,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 45,
                "region_id": 267,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 267,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 45
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;img&gt;\nA flowchart showing the interaction between a Producer, a RabbitMQ broker, and a Consumer. The Producer sends messages to the RabbitMQ broker, which contains three types of exchanges: Direct, Topic, and Fanout. These exchanges are connected to various queues (Queue 1, Queue 2, Q3, Q4, Q5, Q6) via bindings and routing patterns. The Consumer receives messages from the queues.\n&lt;/img&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.258,
                "y": 0.075,
                "width": 0.545,
                "height": 0.20799999999999996,
                "text": "figure",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 45,
                "region_id": 268,
                "type": "figure",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 268,
              "type": "figure",
              "page": 45
            },
            {
              "content": "*Figure 18 An example of RabbitMQ broker and different exchange types.*",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.235,
                "y": 0.298,
                "width": 0.585,
                "height": 0.014000000000000012,
                "text": "caption",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 45,
                "region_id": 269,
                "type": "caption",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 269,
              "type": "caption",
              "page": 45
            },
            {
              "content": "Virtual hosts are used in the RabbitMQ broker to enable hosting multiple isolated environments. Those environments include each their own users, exchanges, queues, and bindings. RabbitMQ cluster contains one or more instances of brokers, which all share exchanges, queues, bindings, virtual hosts, users, and other configurations.\nRabbitMQ implements both push and pull models between broker and consumer, but the documentation says that the push model is highly recommended since the pull model is inefficient. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.341,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.13399999999999995,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 45,
                "region_id": 270,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 270,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 45
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.4.2 Scalability mechanisms",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.493,
                "width": 0.364,
                "height": 0.019000000000000017,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 45,
                "region_id": 271,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 271,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 45
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ scales out by replicating broker instances into master/slave topology and replicating data across them. In case of a master broker failure, the messages will be automatically redirected to slave instances. This is implemented using mirrored queues, which means that the queues from the master broker are replicated to slave brokers. Once the message is acknowledged, the message will be removed from master and slave broker instances.\nIn RabbitMQ cluster, all the users, exchanges, and bindings are by default automatically replicated across the whole cluster. The only exception is the normal queues that only reside only on a single broker instance. In the cluster, which has multiple broker instances, all the queues remain visible and reachable for all broker instances, whether they are located in master or slave brokers. Once the master broker fails, the slave brokers will go through a new master election process and will continue with their new master/slave topology. It is highly advised not to create two-node clusters. Odd numbers should be preferred for master/slave topology to function properly. There is also a concept of shovel and federation to make RabbitMQ brokers distributed.\nThere is also a new approach to mirrored queues called quorum queues, which",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.159,
                "y": 0.531,
                "width": 0.721,
                "height": 0.32599999999999996,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 45,
                "region_id": 272,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 272,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 45
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;40&lt;/page_number&gt;\noffer better throughput and reliability than mirrored queues. Quorum queues retain all the messages in their memory and on disk, meaning they have higher latency than mirrored queues. [20]\nWith mirrored queues, RabbitMQ saves messages in batches to the disk. The interval is on average about a few hundred milliseconds between disk calls. To increase performance, it is suggested that applications should process acknowledgments asynchronously in an unordered fashion. Queues can be assigned a priority property between one to ten. Higher the priority, the faster the message will be processed by the broker. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.04,
                "width": 0.7090000000000001,
                "height": 0.231,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 46,
                "region_id": 273,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 273,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 46
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.4.3 Reliability mechanisms",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.289,
                "width": 0.34500000000000003,
                "height": 0.017000000000000015,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 46,
                "region_id": 274,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 274,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 46
            },
            {
              "content": "In RabbitMQ, one can implement message persistence and durability by leveraging durable queues, exchanges, and persistent messages. In RabbitMQ, durable queues and exchanges mean that they can be recreated automatically after restart. Non-durable queues and exchanges will not be able to be recreated, and persistent messages will have no effect on this type of queues and exchanges. Message persistence mode should be defined in the message by the publisher, simply publishing into a durable queue and exchange will not make the message persistent. [20]\nRabbitMQ leverages acknowledgments in broker-to-consumer messaging to ensure data delivery. In the publisher-to-broker messaging, there is a similar functionality, but it is called a confirm. Using confirms, the broker sends back a confirmation upon receiving the message to the producer. It is being called as confirm and not acknowledgement, since RabbitMQ has made there extensions on top of original AMQP acknowledgment logic. The acknowledgment can be configured to be sent at receiving or after processing the message. The confirm will be sent by the broker when the durable queue has persisted the message to the disk. [20]\nThere is also a concept called Lazy Queues that persist the messages into the storage as soon as possible, minimizing the time they are retained in memory. RabbitMQ cleans the message from the persistent storage periodically using garbage collection after the message is acknowledged. This means that RabbitMQ does not retain the messages after consuming like Kafka and Pulsar. [20]\nMessage delivery can be guaranteed only by using acknowledgments and confirms, and they can be redelivered if acknowledgment is not received. Acknowledgments do not guarantee that the message will not be lost due, e.g., networking error. Redelivery can lead to duplicated messages, meaning RabbitMQ offers at-least-once and at-most-once delivery guarantees.\nRabbitMQ implements a FIFO pattern in its queues, but that is only per queue basis. That means RabbitMQ does not guarantee strict message ordering, and consumers should not rely on the message order. In general, RabbitMQ is very",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.326,
                "width": 0.7090000000000001,
                "height": 0.571,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 46,
                "region_id": 275,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 275,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 46
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;41&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.013000000000000012,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 47,
                "region_id": 276,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 276,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 47
            },
            {
              "content": "reliable and durable, but there are many ways to screw up.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.076,
                "width": 0.514,
                "height": 0.013999999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 47,
                "region_id": 277,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 277,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 47
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ does not have any message encoding features supported. Publishers can still encode the messages before sending them using HTTP supported encoding, such as gzip. The open source version of RabbitMQ does not support inter-cluster message compression, which could greatly decrease the amount of data exchanged in the cluster. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.097,
                "width": 0.717,
                "height": 0.094,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 47,
                "region_id": 278,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 278,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 47
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.4.4 Security mechanisms",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.214,
                "width": 0.33199999999999996,
                "height": 0.015000000000000013,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 47,
                "region_id": 279,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 279,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 47
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ has built-in support for SSL/TLS. RabbitMQ brokers accept connections from clients, other brokers, and administration tools. SSL/TLS can be used to secure all communications by leveraging certificates.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.253,
                "width": 0.717,
                "height": 0.04899999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 47,
                "region_id": 280,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 280,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 47
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ supports multiple authentication mechanisms. RabbitMQ supports SAML authentication by default. There is also username/password-based internal mechanism. Also X.509 certificates can be used. The username/password-based mechanism is managed by the internal authentication backend. Users can also be granted limited access to different virtual hosts, and in them, users can have different kinds of permissions. There is also a possibility to use LDAP through a plugin. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.31,
                "width": 0.717,
                "height": 0.11499999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 47,
                "region_id": 281,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 281,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 47
            },
            {
              "content": "### 4.4.5 Backward compatibility",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.447,
                "width": 0.371,
                "height": 0.015000000000000013,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 47,
                "region_id": 282,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 282,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 47
            },
            {
              "content": "Upgrading RabbitMQ requires checking multiple steps when comparing to Kafka and Pulsar. For some versions, the broker must be first upgraded to an intermediate version to upgrade to the wanted version. RabbitMQ supports rolling update only between specific versions. A rolling update is available in 3.7.18 and latter versions. Earlier versions typically require a full cluster stop.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.481,
                "width": 0.717,
                "height": 0.09399999999999997,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 47,
                "region_id": 283,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 283,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 47
            },
            {
              "content": "It is highly recommended to also upgrade the Erlang version together with RabbitMQ, since certain versions of them are not compatible. In addition, since RabbitMQ supports a large amount of plugins, their interoperability must also be verified. Upgrading a RabbitMQ cluster with multiple broker instances that have different versions is supported only for versions 3.8 and latter. RabbitMQ does not support downgrades. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.161,
                "y": 0.583,
                "width": 0.717,
                "height": 0.11699999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 47,
                "region_id": 284,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 284,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 47
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;42&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.013000000000000012,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 48,
                "region_id": 285,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 285,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 48
            },
            {
              "content": "# 5 Comparison results",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.164,
                "y": 0.105,
                "width": 0.30899999999999994,
                "height": 0.015,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 48,
                "region_id": 286,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 286,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 48
            },
            {
              "content": "Benchmarking results are always in relation to the test execution environment and its performance. The included testbeds are described next per paper. The listing of the papers and all the related information can be found in Section 7.2.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.164,
                "y": 0.143,
                "width": 0.716,
                "height": 0.05200000000000002,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 48,
                "region_id": 287,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 287,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 48
            },
            {
              "content": "## 5.1 Testbeds of each paper",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.164,
                "y": 0.219,
                "width": 0.33299999999999996,
                "height": 0.014000000000000012,
                "text": "title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 48,
                "region_id": 288,
                "type": "title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 288,
              "type": "title",
              "page": 48
            },
            {
              "content": "[SP1] A cluster with 5 brokers with a CPU of 12 cores @ 2300.13Mhz, with 16GB of memory, HDD as a persistent storage and 1Gbps network connection. Single producer and consumer tests are run by scaling node amount from 1 to 5 with an amount of 1 million messages of size 50 Bit each. In case of multiple producers and consumers the node amount is kept constant and the amount of consumers and producers are scaled up. Persistent storage was not attached to RabbitMQ.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.178,
                "y": 0.255,
                "width": 0.651,
                "height": 0.15199999999999997,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 48,
                "region_id": 289,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 289,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 48
            },
            {
              "content": "[SP2] A Linux server with 3.11 kernel, CPU of 24 cores (Intel Xeon X5660 @ 2.80GHz) and 12GB of RAM. The hard disk was WD1003FBYX-01Y7B0 running at 7200 rpm. RabbitMQ version was 3.5.3 and Kafka version 0.10.0.1 with default recommended configurations. New broker instances were always started on the same machine.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.178,
                "y": 0.415,
                "width": 0.651,
                "height": 0.09200000000000003,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 48,
                "region_id": 290,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 290,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 48
            },
            {
              "content": "[SP3] A single machine with CPU of 12 cores @ 3.6Ghz, 16GB of memory and SSD as persistent storage. Kafka version 2.2.1, RabbitMQ 3.8.1, and Pulsar 2.6.0 with their default recommended configurations.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.178,
                "y": 0.516,
                "width": 0.651,
                "height": 0.07399999999999995,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 48,
                "region_id": 291,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 291,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 48
            },
            {
              "content": "[SP4] No information about the hardware, but the tests were carried out for Kafka and RabbitMQ with a single broker on a single machine. No replication was allowed, meaning that writing and reading for Kafka were measured on a single topic and a single partition. For RabbitMQ, the tests were carried out in the same fashion, allowing only one queue with one partition.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.178,
                "y": 0.6,
                "width": 0.651,
                "height": 0.10999999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 48,
                "region_id": 292,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 292,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 48
            },
            {
              "content": "[SP5] Server was deployed to UpCloud with Debian 10 operating system. CPU of 20 Core Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 6136 CPU @ 3.00GHz, memory of 32Gb RAM and 200Gb SSD as persistent storage. The tests were conducted on a single machine. Pulsar and Kafka were both used with their default configuration. Pulsar-client and broker versions 2.5.1 were used. Confluent-kafka was used as a client library with version 1.4.1 and Kafka broker version was 2.5.0.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.178,
                "y": 0.719,
                "width": 0.651,
                "height": 0.14200000000000002,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 48,
                "region_id": 293,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 293,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 48
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;43&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.515,
                "y": 0.046,
                "width": 0.01200000000000001,
                "height": 0.010000000000000002,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 294,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 294,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "[SP6] A server was running in openlandscape.cloud with Ubuntu 16.04LTS as an operating system. Each machine in the cluster had a CPU with eight cores, 16Gb RAM and 240Gb SSD. Kafka version 2.2.0 and Pulsar version 2.3.1. With a configuration to allow accepting large messages.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.215,
                "y": 0.09,
                "width": 0.633,
                "height": 0.097,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 295,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 295,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "## 5.2 Which consumer-producer patterns do Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ implement?",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.201,
                "width": 0.702,
                "height": 0.04199999999999998,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 296,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 296,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "According to study, RabbitMQ allows modifying the exchanges through an API, where as Kafka allows only the producer decide which partition to send the message, hence RabbitMQ has better routing functionalities. [SP2]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.264,
                "width": 0.702,
                "height": 0.05399999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 297,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 297,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "One study found that in terms of message patterns, RabbitMQ and Kafka are equal, but RabbitMQ does not have support for message batching. [SP4]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.325,
                "width": 0.702,
                "height": 0.033999999999999975,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 298,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 298,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "A Study compared push/pull implementations for all the compared technologies. In Kafka, producers push the messages to the broker, and consumers pull them from the broker. In RabbitMQ, the producer pushes messages to an exchange, which delivers them to the queue, and then the broker pushes the messages to the consumers. In Pulsar, the producer pushes the messages to the broker, and then the broker pushes them to the consumer. [SP3]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.366,
                "width": 0.702,
                "height": 0.119,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 299,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 299,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ support point-to-point and publish/subscribe patterns.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.49,
                "width": 0.702,
                "height": 0.03400000000000003,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 300,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 300,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "## 5.3 How do scalability mechanisms differ in Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ?",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.544,
                "width": 0.702,
                "height": 0.04299999999999993,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 301,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 301,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "According to one study, Kafka and RabbitMQ support broker replication. Kafka leverages ZooKeeper, and it is built-in in RabbitMQ. RabbitMQ and Kafka both support master-slave architecture in their cluster. Kafka leverages partitions in data replication, and RabbitMQ mirrored queues. The difference is that new brokers added into the RabbitMQ cluster are by default visible to all the consumers. In Kafka, they are not visible because Kafka's architecture leverages consumer groups which can access the replicas of partitions that they are only assigned to. [SP1]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.608,
                "width": 0.702,
                "height": 0.137,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 302,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 302,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "According to another study, Pulsar has the best scalability due to its three-layered architecture (Pulsar broker, ZooKeeper, BookKeeper) when comparing to Kafka and RabbitMQ. The same study argued that Kafka's scalability is better than RabbitMQ's since Kafka has two-layered architecture (Kafka broker and ZooKeeper). RabbitMQ had the worst scalability of all three. [SP3]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.751,
                "width": 0.702,
                "height": 0.09699999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 303,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 303,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "According to documentation, the Pulsar broker is implemented being stateless, and BookKeeper is stateful, meaning scaling can be done without re-partitioning",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.173,
                "y": 0.855,
                "width": 0.702,
                "height": 0.03500000000000003,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 49,
                "region_id": 304,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 304,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 49
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;44&lt;/page_number&gt;\nexisting data between brokers. Kafka brokers require re-partitioning. The Pulsar cluster supports multitenancy, meaning that multiple users can share a cluster using namespaces like in Kubernetes. Kafka does not support multitenancy. Pulsar is also possible to be integrated with RabbitMQ and Kafka, making it easy to integrate with existing systems. [18] [19]\nWhen it comes to geo-replication, it is built-in in Pulsar, whereas MirrorMaker must be used with Kafka. With RabbitMQ, a plugin should be used to implement geo-replication by shoveling. [18][19][20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.077,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.09499999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 50,
                "region_id": 305,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 305,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 50
            },
            {
              "content": "## 5.4 How does throughput differ in Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ?",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.177,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.057999999999999996,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 50,
                "region_id": 306,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 306,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 50
            },
            {
              "content": "One study found that Kafka’s latency decreased to one-third without any meaningful effect on the producer and consumer throughput when scaling from one to five brokers, one consumer, and producer per broker. The RabbitMQ’s throughput also remained high. Kafka’s latency dropped to one-third when scaling from one to five brokers. The study did not provide results for RabbitMQ’s latency with a single producer and consumer per broker. [SP1] The same study found that latency in Kafka increased more than two orders of magnitude when an increasing amount of consumers and producers from one to five on five separate brokers, hence incoming traffic increasing 5x. The same study found that Kafka’s producer throughput dropped about 20% and consumer throughput dropped 96The study found in the same test that the producer throughput about 75% and consumer throughput dropped about 10% in RabbitMQ. RabbitMQ’s latency stayed low until it started increasing sharply after adding more than 15 consumers and producers, causing the queues to fill up, increasing latency. [SP1]\nOne study found that both, RabbitMQ and Kafka can deliver millisecond-level low latencies when using at-most-once when both read only from internal memory. Replication did not drastically affect the results. The same study found that when using at-least-once, RabbitMQ’s latency was not really impacted when comparing to at-most-once. This result was argued to be a result of RabbitMQ retaining messages quite long in the memory, meaning that even if messages are written to the disk, they are also available in memory. The results show also that RabbitMQ’s latency remains around 10ms in both message delivery guarantees. Kafka’s latency remains with at-most-once in below 10 ms but increases to around 20ms when using at-least-once. Also, the results show that when reading from disk, the latency can grow up to 100 ms. [SP2] The study found that when using single node, single producer and consumer, single partition, and no replication, RabbitMQ outperforms Kafka. They also argued that, when replication is allowed, Kafka will outperform RabbitMQ. The",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.253,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.04299999999999998,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 50,
                "region_id": 307,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 307,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 50
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;45&lt;/page_number&gt;\nsame study found that RabbitMQ’s throughput decreased by 50% when changing from at-most-once to at-least-once delivery guarantee when replication is allowed. The study also found that Kafka’s throughput decreased by 50% to 75% when changing from at-most-once to at-least-once delivery guarantee when replication is allowed. [SP2]\nOne study argued that Kafka had the best throughput, Pulsar and RabbitMQ had somewhat equal throughput. [SP3]\nAnother study measured the relation between latency and throughput with varying message size and traffic. The study shows, Kafka overperforms RabbitMQ with 1 KB messages by around twice higher writing speed per second. In reading speed, Kafka outperforms RabbitMQ clearly with 3x to 10x better performance. The average latency was overall higher with Kafka than RabbitMQ. The same study shows that with 100 KB messages, RabbitMQ outperforms Kafka clearly in writing speed with 1000 messages per second, but otherwise, there were no significant differences in throughput between them.
