The latency with Kafka was still around 10x higher than RabbitMQ. [SP4]\nOne paper studied Pulsar’s and Kafka’s maximum throughput with message sizes of 1 KB, 65 KB, and 600 KB. The study found out that Pulsar had the highest maximum throughput with 1 KB messages. Kafka had nearly 35% lower maximum throughput with 1 KB messages. When using 65 KB messages, the study found instead that Kafka had 60% higher maximum throughput than Pulsar. The study argued that the result could be a result of a bottleneck in the Pulsar cluster. With 600 KB messages, Kafka outperformed Pulsar again with three times greater throughput. Kafka handled 870 messages/sec and Pulsar 293 messages/sec. In the test, the Pulsar cluster crashed with out-of-memory error and killed the BookKeeper node. This result shows that with a default configuration, the BookKeeper node is not able to handle big messages. This argument was based on the result that when using a non-persistent configuration, Pulsar was able to handle 600 KB messages with a maximum throughput of 2764 messages/sec. [SP5]\nIn the same paper, latency was also measured with varying message size and throughput. Messages of 1 KB were used with 1000 and 5000 messages/sec throughput. Messages of 65 KB were used with 100 and 500 messages/sec throughput. Lastly, messages of 600 KB were used with 50 and 200 messages/sec throughput. With 1 KB messages with both throughput rates, Pulsar’s latency was around 1/4 of Kafka’s. Pulsar managed sub-millisecond latencies with both throughput rates. With 65 KB messages, Pulsar’s latency was half of Kafka’s with a lower throughput rate, and with a higher throughput rate, Pulsar’s latency was about 1/5 of Kafka’s latency. Even though Kafka’s throughput was significantly greater than Pulsar’s, like mentioned earlier with message size of 600 KB, Pulsar’s latency re-",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.169,
                "y": 0.042,
                "width": 0.711,
                "height": 0.846,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 51,
                "region_id": 308,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 308,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 51
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;46&lt;/page_number&gt;\nmains about half of Kafka's with both throughput rates. Kafka's maximum latency was 4-5x greater than Pulsar's. The study concluded that Kafka is the most reliable in terms of maximum throughput in their research. Still, if the Pulsar cluster would be more scaled out, the results could be significantly different. In latency, Pulsar outperformed Kafka in every aspect. [SP5]\nOne paper conducted testing with messages of size 1 KB and 1 MB between Kafka and Pulsar in terms of throughput, latency, ram usage, disk usage and bandwidth usage. The study concluded that Pulsar overperforms Kafka in all aspects of performance. With 1 KB messages, Pulsar had over twice greater throughput, and latency was 15 times smaller than Kafka's. In terms of CPU, RAM, and bandwidth usage, Pulsar outperformed Kafka in all aspects, except Kafka slightly won in overall CPU usage of the whole cluster. With 1 MB messages, the gap decreased between them. Pulsar had around 27% higher throughput, but Pulsar's latency was still around 15 times smaller than Kafka's. Also, in terms of CPU, RAM, and bandwidth usage, Pulsar outperformed Kafka in all aspects, except disk writing speed. The study concluded that Kafka still has greater community support and a rich ecosystem, hence making it still a good choice. [SP6]\nKafka supports compression with Gzip, Snappy,14z, and Zstandard. [18] Pulsar supports zlib, Snappy,14z, and Zstandard. [19] RabbitMQ does not support any compression algorithms since AMQP does not support them. Still, some compression can be done on the client side using default HTTP supported compression, such as Gzip. RabbitMQ does not support inter-node message compression in its open source version. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.03,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.508,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 52,
                "region_id": 309,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 309,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 52
            },
            {
              "content": "## 5.5 How do reliability mechanisms differ in Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ?",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.555,
                "width": 0.719,
                "height": 0.04399999999999993,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 52,
                "region_id": 310,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 310,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 52
            },
            {
              "content": "A study found that if reliability and data safety is essential, such that losing a single message can be critical, RabbitMQ is a better choice than Kafka. RabbitMQ and its AMQP protocol have a wider range of security features and still can maintain a good performance when comparing to Kafka. [SP1]\nAccording to a study, RabbitMQ has a feature to sort messages sent into a queue, whereas Kafka can only retain order per partition. Since Kafka leverages heavily batching and aims for high throughput, it is designed such that the whole batch must be resent to correct the ordering in the partition. RabbitMQ can correct that ordering in the queue by leveraging acknowledgments, and since messages are by default retained in memory, there's no need to resend a full batch. [SP2]\nA study argued that Pulsar had the best ordering guarantee when compared to RabbitMQ and Kafka, Kafka had the second-best ordering guarantee, and Rab-",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.623,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.245,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 52,
                "region_id": 311,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 311,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 52
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;47&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.513,
                "y": 0.046,
                "width": 0.015000000000000013,
                "height": 0.010000000000000002,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 312,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 312,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "bitMQ the worst. The same study argued that there were no significant differences in delivery guarantees between all three systems, even though RabbitMQ does not implement exactly-one guarantee. The same study argued also, that overall reliability was the best in RabbitMQ, and Pulsar and Rabbit just a little bit worse. [SP3]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.09,
                "width": 0.71,
                "height": 0.097,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 313,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 313,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "One study concluded that in terms of reliability, Kafka is more reliable than RabbitMQ since it has more features it supports. The main difference the study found is that RabbitMQ does not support replication synchronization and idempotent messaging. [SP4]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.193,
                "width": 0.71,
                "height": 0.07700000000000001,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 314,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 314,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "## 5.6 Which security mechanisms do Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ support?",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.29,
                "width": 0.71,
                "height": 0.04200000000000004,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 315,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 315,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka supports SSL/TLS encryption in broker-client, broker-broker, broker-ZooKeeper and broker-cli connections. Authentication can be done in Kafka using between them using SSL/TLS, SASL, and Kerberos. Authorization is done in Kafka using ACLs. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.352,
                "width": 0.726,
                "height": 0.07600000000000001,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 316,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 316,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "Pulsar supports Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) end-to-end encryption from client to storage level. Pulsar also supports SSL/TLS to encrypt messaging between client-broker, broker-broker, and broker-cli. Authentication can be done in Pulsar using SSL/TLS, Athenz, Kerberos, SASL, and JSON WTA. Authorization is done in Pulsar using ACLs or network segmentation. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.435,
                "width": 0.71,
                "height": 0.09600000000000003,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 317,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 317,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ supports encrypting messages with SSL/TLS between client-broker, broker-broker, and broker-client. Two primary authentication mechanisms for clients are username/password and X.509 certificates (SSL/TLS), but RabbitMQ also supports SASL. RabbitMQ can implement authorization using separate virtual hosts assigned to different users. Also, ACL-type of permission list can be used in those virtual hosts to grant or deny operations. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.537,
                "width": 0.71,
                "height": 0.11699999999999999,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 318,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 318,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "## 5.7 How does backward compatibility differ in Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ?",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.674,
                "width": 0.71,
                "height": 0.041999999999999926,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 319,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 319,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka broker is backward compatible and supports rolling update. Client versions are forward compatible. Documentation does not describe in detail the actual implementation of the rolling update, what kind of effects that have on partitioning and assigned clients. Downgrading is supported if something goes wrong in the rolling update. [18]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.736,
                "width": 0.71,
                "height": 0.09699999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 320,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 320,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "According to Pulsar's documentation, broker and client versions are backward compatible as well as forward compatible. The main benefit that already was mentioned in regards to upgradability is that the Pulsar broker is stateless. By storing",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.172,
                "y": 0.84,
                "width": 0.71,
                "height": 0.05500000000000005,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 53,
                "region_id": 321,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 321,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 53
            },
            {
              "content": "48",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.5139056831922612,
                "y": 0.04489097905087644,
                "width": 0.019951632406287785,
                "height": 0.010688328345446772,
                "text": "number",
                "confidence": 0.9008,
                "page": 54,
                "region_id": 322,
                "type": "number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 322,
              "type": "number",
              "page": 54
            },
            {
              "content": "data in BookKeeper, the brokers can be upgraded without a need for re-partitioning.\nPulsar supports rolling update and downgrade in case of an error. [19]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.1626360338573156,
                "y": 0.09020949123557075,
                "width": 0.717049576783555,
                "height": 0.03505771697306541,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 0.9614,
                "page": 54,
                "region_id": 323,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 323,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 54
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ supports rolling update with newer versions, but it is only possible between certain versions if update is done from 3.7.18 version or earlier. Sometimes with older versions, updating between the wanted versions requires an update to an intermediate version first. It is recommended also to upgrade the Erlang version with the broker, and on top of that, the certain broker versions and Erlang versions are not compatible. Upgrading a RabbitMQ cluster with multiple versions of broken instances is supported only for versions 3.8 and latter. RabbitMQ does not support downgrades. [20]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.1626360338573156,
                "y": 0.13125267208208635,
                "width": 0.7182587666263603,
                "height": 0.15904232578024796,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 0.9873,
                "page": 54,
                "region_id": 324,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 324,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 54
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;49&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.014000000000000012,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 55,
                "region_id": 325,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 325,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 55
            },
            {
              "content": "# 6 Discussion",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.106,
                "width": 0.19299999999999998,
                "height": 0.016,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 55,
                "region_id": 326,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 326,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 55
            },
            {
              "content": "Comparing these messaging systems by the defined characteristics, some certain differences can be found. For example, using persistent storage is possible in each technology but not required in RabbitMQ. Out of all three, RabbitMQ implements many things differently since it is a traditional message broker, not aiming for high throughput. Kafka and Pulsar are quite similar in how they handle data. Pulsar and Kafka’s main difference is that Pulsar uses a separate data storage system called BookKeeper, and Pulsar has tiered storage. This three-layered architecture with ZooKeeper and BookKeeper increases scalability by enabling the Pulsar broker’s statelessness compared to Kafka’s two-layered architecture and its stateful broker. Also, some research shows that Pulsar with proper configuration can outperform Kafka in nearly all of the performance-related aspects, including throughput and latency [SP6]. Also, Pulsar’s end-to-end encryption with AES, built-in geo-replication, and multi-tenancy is something Kafka does not support by default. Pulsar supports a wider amount of authentication mechanisms and authorization mechanisms when comparing to Kafka.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.143,
                "width": 0.716,
                "height": 0.30000000000000004,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 55,
                "region_id": 327,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 327,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 55
            },
            {
              "content": "The best choice as a MOM is always use case specific. Meaning, testing should always be done before jumping into conclusions, simply based on some results that can be read on the site of one solution. Typically, every MOM tries to market their own solution on their site and show test results in use cases in which perform well.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.447,
                "width": 0.716,
                "height": 0.07300000000000001,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 55,
                "region_id": 328,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 328,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 55
            },
            {
              "content": "Where Pulsar shines in performance and scalability, it still lacks some community support and technological maturity when comparing to Kafka. The technological maturity will probably come with the time, but the future will show how it gets adapted from the community. In Table 3, some information is gathered from repositories of Kafka, Pulsar, and RabbitMQ on Github.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.526,
                "width": 0.716,
                "height": 0.09999999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 55,
                "region_id": 329,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 329,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 55
            },
            {
              "content": "<table>\n  <caption>Table 3 Repository comparison of the chosen technologies.</caption>\n  <thead>\n    <tr>\n      <th></th>\n      <th>apache/kafka</th>\n      <th>apache/pulsar</th>\n      <th>rabbitmq/rabbitmq-server</th>\n    </tr>\n  </thead>\n  <tbody>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Stars</td>\n      <td>18189</td>\n      <td>7411</td>\n      <td>8148</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Forks</td>\n      <td>9676</td>\n      <td>1865</td>\n      <td>3067</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Open Issues</td>\n      <td>807</td>\n      <td>1163</td>\n      <td>217</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Age</td>\n      <td>10 years</td>\n      <td>5 years</td>\n      <td>10 years</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Language</td>\n      <td>Java, Scala</td>\n      <td>Java</td>\n      <td>Erlang</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>License</td>\n      <td>Apache-2.0</td>\n      <td>Apache-2.0</td>\n      <td>Mozilla Public License 2.0</td>\n    </tr>\n  </tbody>\n</table>",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.643,
                "width": 0.716,
                "height": 0.16900000000000004,
                "text": "table",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 55,
                "region_id": 330,
                "type": "table",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 330,
              "type": "table",
              "page": 55
            },
            {
              "content": "There exist many hosted offerings of the chosen technologies. Confluent offers Kafka commercially to users using their Confluent Platform and Confluent Cloud.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.841,
                "width": 0.716,
                "height": 0.038000000000000034,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 55,
                "region_id": 331,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 331,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 55
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;50&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.015000000000000013,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 56,
                "region_id": 332,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 332,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 56
            },
            {
              "content": "RabbitMQ is hosted by Cloud AMQP and Cloud Foundry. Pulsar is currently hosted in Alibaba Cloud, called Pulsar-as-a-service, and StreamNative also has their offering for Pulsar in their cloud.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.077,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.05500000000000001,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 56,
                "region_id": 333,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 333,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 56
            },
            {
              "content": "Confluent currently works on the 'Next-Generation event streaming platform' called Project Metamorphosis, which aims to lower cost and better performance than their current Kafka offering. The future will show whether it will be open sourced or not. [22]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.137,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.07399999999999998,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 56,
                "region_id": 334,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 334,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 56
            },
            {
              "content": "There is also some effort being put into a general benchmarking framework to overcome biased results when benchmarking MOMs. It is called OpenMessaging Benchmark Framework. It is currently a Linux Foundation Collaborative Project and is being supported, for example, by Alibaba. According to README.md in their Github repository, they support benchmarking for Kafka, RocketMQ, RabbitMQ, Pulsar, and NATS Streaming. [23]",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.16,
                "y": 0.22,
                "width": 0.72,
                "height": 0.11800000000000002,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 56,
                "region_id": 335,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 335,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 56
            },
            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;51&lt;/page_number&gt;",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.518,
                "y": 0.035,
                "width": 0.014000000000000012,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 57,
                "region_id": 336,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 336,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 57
            },
            {
              "content": "# 7 Conclusion",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.105,
                "width": 0.19999999999999998,
                "height": 0.017,
                "text": "paragraph_title",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 57,
                "region_id": 337,
                "type": "paragraph_title",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 337,
              "type": "paragraph_title",
              "page": 57
            },
            {
              "content": "Kafka remains the number one solution for most event streaming applications that demand high throughput due to its rich ecosystem and wide community adoption. RabbitMQ is the choice for reliable and safe messaging. Pulsar is something to keep an eye on. It could be replacing Kafka if it gets adopted by the community. Pulsar is a great tool for a software architect to have as a Kafka alternative. Table 4 in Chapter 7.2 summarizes all three technologies.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.143,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.11700000000000002,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 57,
                "region_id": 338,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 338,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 57
            },
            {
              "content": "When selecting papers for the research, it was hard to find papers that would be comparable. Usually, papers only concerned with a single technology with varying testbeds, meaning they did not provide any value to the comparison. In some of the papers, there is sometimes clear false information to one's eye. That is maybe due to updates to the systems or just general differences in opinions. Meaning that one could, for example, argue that RabbitMQ implements batching using 'consumer prefetch'. It is not actually batching but kind of close, so it is not really a false argument. Or one could argue that Kafka does not implement exactly-once message delivery because using it would 'kill the performance'. Implementing it is technically possible but complicated. These opinion differences allow the firms behind each technology to make some immoral marketing arguments. Also, some chosen papers did not describe the testbed or hardware in detail enough to make the tests fully comparable. Any of the papers did not compare security or backward compatibility related functionality in detail.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,
                "y": 0.265,
                "width": 0.718,
                "height": 0.28,
                "text": "text",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 57,
                "region_id": 339,
                "type": "text",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 339,
              "type": "text",
              "page": 57
            },
            {
              "content": "For future work, some comparisons should be done about how authentication and encryption mechanisms affect throughput and latency. Also, some research should be done about finding optimal hardware configurations such as CPU speed and memory size. As technologies evolve, general throughput and latency benchmarking continue to be also relevant. These tests should be done using OpenMessaging Benchmark Framework to maintain comparability in results.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.162,

            {
              "content": "&lt;page_number&gt;53&lt;/page_number&gt;\nTable 4 Comparison table of the chosen technologies.",
              "bounding_box": {
                "x": 0.519,
                "y": 0.034,
                "width": 0.01200000000000001,
                "height": 0.009999999999999995,
                "text": "page_number",
                "confidence": 1.0,
                "page": 59,
                "region_id": 345,
                "type": "page_number",
                "normalized": true,
                "image_dimensions": {
                  "width": 1654,
                  "height": 2339
                }
              },
              "region_id": 345,
              "type": "page_number",
              "page": 59
            },
            {
              "content": "<table>\n  <thead>\n    <tr>\n      <th></th>\n      <th>Kafka</th>\n      <th>Pulsar</th>\n      <th>RabbitMQ</th>\n    </tr>\n  </thead>\n  <tbody>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Developed aiming for</td>\n      <td>High throughput and low latency</td>\n      <td>High throughput and low latency</td>\n      <td>Reliability and data safety</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Messaging patterns</td>\n      <td>Point-to-Point, Pub/Sub</td>\n      <td>Point-to-Point, Pub/Sub</td>\n      <td>Point-to-Point, Pub/Sub</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Push or Pull with consumer</td>\n      <td>Pull</td>\n      <td>Push, API simulates pull</td>\n      <td>Both, but push preferred</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Message durability</td>\n      <td>Yes</td>\n      <td>Yes</td>\n      <td>By using durable queues</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Message persistence</td>\n      <td>Yes</td>\n      <td>Yes, Also tiered storage</td>\n      <td>Possible, but does not by default have it</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Message reliability mechanism</td>\n      <td>Acknowledgements</td>\n      <td>Acknowledgements</td>\n      <td>Acknowledgements and confirms</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Message delivery guarantees</td>\n      <td>At-least-once, At-most-once, Exactly-once</td>\n      <td>At-least-once, At-most-once, Effectively-once</td>\n      <td>At-least-once, At-most-once</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Message ordering</td>\n      <td>Per partition</td>\n      <td>Per partition with partitioned topics</td>\n      <td>Per queue</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Data replication mechanism</td>\n      <td>Partitions in a log</td>\n      <td>Bookies, partitions and segments</td>\n      <td>Mirrored Queues, Quorum Queues</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Broker replication mechanism</td>\n      <td>ZooKeeper handles replication</td>\n      <td>ZooKeeper handles replication</td>\n      <td>Build-in replication, Shoveling, Federation</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Geo-replication</td>\n      <td>Using MirrorMaker</td>\n      <td>Built-in functionality</td>\n      <td>Hard to implement</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Message batching</td>\n      <td>Yes</td>\n      <td>Yes</td>\n      <td>Hard to implement</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Supports streaming</td>\n      <td>Yes</td>\n      <td>Yes</td>\n      <td>No</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Smart broker, dumb client</td>\n      <td>No</td>\n      <td>No</td>\n      <td>Yes</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Compression types</td>\n      <td>Gzip, Snappy, l4z and Zstandard</td>\n      <td>Zlib, Snappy, l4z and Zstandard</td>\n      <td>None</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Encryption</td>\n      <td>SSL/TLS</td>\n      <td>SSL/TLS, AES</td>\n      <td>SSL/TLS</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Authentication</td>\n      <td>SSL/TLS, SASL, Kerberos</td>\n      <td>SSL/TLS, Athenz, Kerberos, SASL, JSON WTA</td>\n      <td>SSL/TLS, SASL, username and password</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Authorization</td>\n      <td>ACL</td>\n      <td>Permissions, Tokens</td>\n      <td>vHosts, Permissions</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Backward compatibility</td>\n      <td>Good</td>\n      <td>Excellent</td>\n      <td>Only between certain versions</td>\n    </tr>\n    <tr>\n      <td>Rolling udpate</td>\n      <td>Supported</td>\n      <td>Supported</td>\n      <td>Possible but complicated</td>\n    </tr>\n  </tbody>\n</table>",
